text: The surgeon applies tape to both buttocks near the edges of the cleft marking and draws them apart, opening the cleft like a fan with the apex serving as the hinge.
The surgeon pulls the tape laterally and encephalad to open the cleft nearest the anus, enhancing visualization and mobilization of the skin around open wounds and palenital wounds close to the anus.
The previously parallel inked lines marking the cleft edges are now visible forming an inverted V shape, with all other markings removed for clarity.
The surgeon shaves the cleft area, removing any residual hair, including from the perianal skin.
The surgeon prepares to remove skin to make the cleft shallower and flatter, focusing on the area marked by the inked lines.
The surgeon removes skin from one side of the cleft and drapes skin from the opposite side across the midline to cover the exposed area, creating an incision that lies away from the midline.
The surgeon marks the skin nearly perpendicular to the midline after passing the last open wound, then curves the marking radially towards the ten o'clock or two o'clock position.

The surgeon uses a pen to draw an incision line radiating from the anus, following a contour that approaches the right safety line. A lateral line is drawn parallel to the first line. Marcane with epinephrine is applied to the area to reduce intraoperative bleeding and prevent postoperative hematoma formation.
The surgeon makes the first incision, removing all damaged skin, cutting down to the subcutaneous tissue layer.
The surgeon incises the flap out to the safety lines, applying upward traction to avoid creating a buttonhole in the skin flap.

The surgeon undermines the skin, detaching it from the superficial external sphincter fibers, causing the skin tethered deep in the cleft to elevate and rotate away from the deep cleft. The underlying soft tissue, now free from the skin, naturally falls together to form a pad that prevents the skin from returning to the cleft.
The surgeon undermines the flap of skin to be excised, extending the dissection out to the predetermined safety lines.
The surgeon carefully excises minimal soft tissue, maintaining a thin flap to ensure adequate soft tissue remains in the cleft, allowing it to naturally fall together once the tapes are removed.
The surgeon sweeps away granulation tissue lining the pilonidal cavity, revealing healthy, well-vascularized scar tissue beneath.

The surgeon incises the tissue to release tension and minimize the defect, revealing dense cubes of scar tissue. These will aid in approximating soft tissue and closing the dead space. The tapes are then removed.
The surgeon removes the tapes from the buttocks, releasing considerable tension and facilitating a tension-free repair.

With the tapes removed, the surgeon gently drapes the preserved skin flap across the midline to the safety line on the opposite side, ensuring it reaches without tension. The flap of skin on the contralateral side is then excised.

The surgeon places a closed suction drain deep within the wound and approximates the midline soft tissue over the drain using PDS sutures in multiple layers. Vessel loops are also used as unobtrusive drains, typically removed one to three days post-surgery.
The surgeon places twenty to thirty soft tissue sutures to approximate the tissue lines, aiming to prevent seroma and hematoma formation.
The surgeon begins placing interrupted PDS sutures, starting from the deeper layers and working outward to approximate the soft tissue.
The surgeon places approximately twenty to thirty sutures to approximate the dead space, aiming to prevent hematoma and seroma formation.
The surgeon places drains either deep near the sacrum or just beneath the skin flap after approximating the cleft.

Near the anus, where the skin rotates more significantly, the surgeon places a dermal stitch between the side wall. Additional subcutaneous and dermal stitches are placed to ensure proper orientation of the cleft before closing the skin.
The surgeon performs a subcuticular closure using monocryl sutures to approximate the skin edges, ensuring a smooth and even closure.

The cleft appears flat in two dimensions, with the raw skin radiating away from the anus shifted to the right. The incisions radiating away from the anus are visible and resemble those made during hemorrhoidectomy.
